results.possibleIdentity: Paul R. Kramer (1935–2018) was a physicist and educator who specialized in instructional technology and computer-assisted education. In the late 1960s, he served as an Assistant Professor of Physics at the State University of New York (SUNY) at Stony Brook. During this time, he was actively involved in exploring the intersection of computers, cybernetics, and media, contributing to the development of computer-assisted instruction (CAI) and 'New Media' in higher education. He was specifically noted for developing computer-based problem drill systems for university courses and was associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that facilitated collaborations between artists and technical professionals. His technical expertise included mathematics, physics, programming, computer graphics, film, and relay control and sequencing—skills that made him a valuable technical resource for the art-and-technology movement of the era. In 1969, he joined the faculty of Farmingdale State College (also part of the SUNY system), where he remained for over 40 years. At Farmingdale, he served as the Chair of the Department of Physics (1977–1981), Dean of Student Services, and was a prominent advocate for academic computing and early adoption of Apple computers on campus. He was a long-time resident of Port Washington, New York.
